summary: O'Neil eviscerates a Wall Street Journal editorial attacking Obama's Pay As You Earn student loan program, calling it vitriolic and illogical. She argues the piece fails to advance debate and misrepresents who benefits from loan forgiveness (including police, healthcare workers, and military, not just government employees). O'Neil proposes free state schools as a real solution to break the feedback loop of rising tuition and student debt. She concludes by pointing out the editorial's logical incoherence: claiming that pushing people toward high-paying private jobs will reduce income inequality contradicts basic economics.
key_points: The WSJ editorial mischaracterizes loan forgiveness beneficiaries, omitting that police, healthcare workers, and military personnel also qualify
Rising college tuition is a long-standing problem driven by a feedback loop between available loans and schools raising prices to match students' ability to pay
Free state schools would be a real solution, representing an investment in the future and addressing mobility problems
The editorial's claim that encouraging high-paying private sector jobs reduces inequality is logically incoherent
Declining state funding for education, not federal loan programs, is a core driver of the tuition crisis
money_quotes: I just don't understand how an article like this can be published in a respectable newspaper. It does not advance the debate.
It's not just that tuition has been going up, at least at state school. It's that state funding has been going down.
What we have here is a feedback loop, and it's hard to break.
My personal approach would be to make state schools free to make the overall field competitive for college costs.
It's called an investment in our future, and it also would help with the mobility problems we have.
At some point I'm sure it seemed like a terrible idea to form a public elementary school system for free, but we don't think so now.
The claim that by encouraging a bunch of people to go into super high paying private jobs we will reduce income inequality is just weird.
I say if you are so gung-ho about private high-paying jobs, then you also need to embrace rising inequality. Do it in the name of free-market capitalism or something, but please stay logical.
key_insight: Critiquing student loan policy while ignoring declining state education funding and the feedback loop between loans and tuition is intellectually dishonest—and claiming high-paying private jobs reduce inequality is logically absurd.
